event: Early hominins inhabit the Nile Valley, leaving Acheulean stone tools in Egypt's deserts and along the river.
Anatomically modern humans move through the Nile corridor as a route out of Africa during the Middle Stone Age.
Hunter-gatherers and early herders settle the Sahara and Nile during a wetter Holocene climate phase.
Neolithic farming communities such as the Fayum and Merimde cultures establish agriculture and pottery in the Nile region.
The Naqada culture develops complex society, trade, and the early use of hieroglyphic symbols in Upper Egypt.
King Narmer unifies Upper and Lower Egypt, founding the First Dynasty and the dynastic state.
The Old Kingdom begins, ushering in the age of powerful god-kings and monumental stone architecture.
Architect Imhotep builds the Step Pyramid at Saqqara for Pharaoh Djoser, Egypt's first large stone pyramid.
Pharaoh Khufu completes the Great Pyramid of Giza, the largest of the pyramids and a wonder of the ancient world.
The Old Kingdom collapses into the fragmented First Intermediate Period amid drought and weakened central rule.
Mentuhotep II reunifies Egypt, launching the prosperous Middle Kingdom.
The Hyksos, rulers of West Asian origin, seize power in the Delta during the Second Intermediate Period.
Ahmose I expels the Hyksos and founds the New Kingdom, Egypt's imperial golden age.
Hatshepsut becomes one of Egypt's most successful female pharaohs, expanding trade to the land of Punt.
Thutmose III wins the Battle of Megiddo, extending Egyptian power across the Levant.
Akhenaten introduces worship of the sun-disk Aten and moves the capital to Amarna.
The young pharaoh Tutankhamun dies and is buried in a tomb later found nearly intact in 1922.
Ramesses II fights the Hittites at the Battle of Kadesh, leading to one of history's first peace treaties.
The New Kingdom ends and central authority fractures during the Third Intermediate Period.
Kushite kings from Nubia conquer Egypt and rule as the 25th Dynasty.
The Assyrian Empire invades Egypt, sacking Memphis and ending Kushite control.
Persian king Cambyses II conquers Egypt, making it a satrapy of the Achaemenid Empire.
Alexander the Great takes Egypt from Persia and is welcomed as a liberator, founding Alexandria.
Ptolemy I establishes the Greek Ptolemaic dynasty, ruling Egypt from Alexandria.
Priests issue the decree later inscribed on the Rosetta Stone, key to deciphering hieroglyphs.
Cleopatra VII dies after defeat at Actium, and Egypt becomes a province of the Roman Empire.
Emperor Theodosius I bans pagan worship, hastening the closure of ancient Egyptian temples.
The Coptic Church breaks from imperial Christianity over the Council of Chalcedon, shaping Egyptian Christianity.
Arab Muslim armies under Amr ibn al-As conquer Egypt, beginning its Islamization and Arabization.
The Fatimid dynasty conquers Egypt and founds Cairo as its new capital.
Construction of al-Azhar mosque begins in Cairo, becoming a renowned center of Islamic learning.
Saladin overthrows the Fatimids and founds the Ayyubid dynasty, leading wars against the Crusaders.
The Mamluks, a military slave caste, seize power and establish a sultanate ruling Egypt and Syria.
Mamluk forces defeat the Mongols at Ain Jalut, halting their advance into Africa.
The Ottoman Empire conquers Egypt, ending the Mamluk Sultanate and ruling through governors.
Napoleon Bonaparte invades Egypt, bringing scholars whose work launches modern Egyptology.
Muhammad Ali seizes power and founds a dynasty, modernizing Egypt into a near-autonomous state.
The Suez Canal opens, linking the Mediterranean and Red Seas and transforming global trade.
Britain invades and occupies Egypt after the Urabi revolt, beginning de facto colonial control.
Egypt gains nominal independence from Britain as a kingdom under Fuad I.
Hassan al-Banna founds the Muslim Brotherhood in Ismailia, a movement of lasting influence.
Egypt becomes a founding member of the United Nations and the Arab League.
The Free Officers led by Nasser overthrow King Farouk in a military coup.
Egypt is declared a republic, ending the monarchy.
Nasser nationalizes the Suez Canal, triggering the Suez Crisis and an Anglo-French-Israeli invasion.
Egypt suffers a heavy defeat in the Six-Day War, losing the Sinai Peninsula to Israel.
Nasser dies and Anwar Sadat becomes president as the Aswan High Dam is completed.
Egypt launches the October War against Israel, restoring national pride and reshaping diplomacy.
Sadat signs the Egypt-Israel Peace Treaty at Camp David, the first Arab state to recognize Israel.
Sadat is assassinated by Islamist militants, and Hosni Mubarak becomes president.
The new Bibliotheca Alexandrina opens, reviving the legacy of the ancient Library of Alexandria.
Mass protests of the Arab Spring force Mubarak to resign after nearly thirty years in power.
Mohamed Morsi of the Muslim Brotherhood is elected Egypt's first freely chosen president.
The military, led by Abdel Fattah el-Sisi, removes Morsi amid mass protests.
El-Sisi is elected president, consolidating military-backed rule.
Egypt inaugurates a major expansion of the Suez Canal to boost shipping capacity.
Egypt joins the BRICS bloc as it seeks to expand its global economic partnerships.
